MONKEY-BAY, Malawi (Planet Defence) - The USAID funded Restoring Fisheries for Sustainable Livelihoods in Lake Malawi (REFRESH) a five-year project (October 2019 – September 2024) in its quest to continue building constituencies of champions organised an exchange visit for fish processors from Nkhotakota and Salima districts to appreciate Nyampesi Fish Products Company and Fish Landladies all based at Monkey Bay, Mangochi.

Speaking during the exchange visit Hamis Nyampesi, Managing Director of  Nyampesi Fish Products began by explaining his company profile saying it was established in 2016 to buy and process fish using environmental friendly processing technologies.

 The aim was to conserve fish biodiversity while promoting both women and youth empowerment economically.

"At Nyampesi Fish Products Company, we believe in working with youths and women in our fish processing business. That's why we have a business partnership with Fishland Ladies,” explained the youthful owner of the fish processing outlet.

According to Nyampesi his company has received tremendous overwhelming technical support from the then Fisheries Integration of Society and Habitats (FISH) project as well as financial support from the current REFRESH project being implemented in the district.  

"In order to process our fish, we use Chitofu 3 in 1 which is used for frying, parboiling as well as smoking.   The solar tent drier helps in adding value to the fish, since there is no contamination with flies which lead to post harvest loses and further infestations." Nyampesi told the visiting processors at his base.

In her turn, Martha Chikwezga, Fishland Ladies Treasurer pointed out that as a young women led cooperative, they have been receiving a lot of support from Nyampesi Fish Products Company and Department of Fisheries. This is what catapulted them into adopting new fish processing technologies which help to conserve both the environment and fisheries resources. 

"With our privately owned boat, we have employed men who do fishing for our cooperative. After we process the fish, we sell to Nyampesi Fish Products Company as our off – taker at good prices and we really thank him for that,” Chikwezga told the visiting fish processors.

Bahat Stambuli a representative of the visiting fish processors, thanked Pact Malawi for organising such an important exchange visit saying it has helped them know the modern fish processing technologies citing the chitofu 3 in 1,m she said adding that, "we will do everything possible that our cooperative partner with Nyampesi can realise more income from our fish processing business.”

Neverson Msusa, Mangochi  District Fisheries Officer (DFO) in his remarks thanked REFRESH project for funding the event. He added that through such efforts it has enabled fish processors to interact and share ideas on how best to use modern fish processing technologies which will help conserve the environment including fisheries resources. 

REFRESH project is implemented with funding from USAID through Pact Malawi and Mangochi is one of the beneficiary district and implemented by partner Community Initiative for Self-Reliance (CISER).

MONKEY-BAY, Malawi (Planet Defence) - The USAID-funded Restoring Fisheries for Sustainable Livelihoods in Lake Malawi (REFRESH) project, which spans five years from October 2019 to September 2024, recently organized an exchange visit for fish processors from Nkhotakota and Salima districts. The purpose of the visit was to provide them with an opportunity to understand and appreciate the operations of Nyampesi Fish Products Company and Fish Landladies, both located in Monkey Bay, Mangochi.

During the exchange visit, Hamis Nyampesi, the Managing Director of Nyampesi Fish Products, began by sharing information about his company. Established in 2016, their primary goal is to purchase and process fish using environmentally-friendly techniques. They aim to not only conserve fish biodiversity but also promote economic empowerment, particularly among women and youth.

"Our commitment at Nyampesi Fish Products Company is to collaborate with young people and women in our fish processing business. That's why we have formed a business partnership with Fishland Ladies," explained the enthusiastic owner of the fish processing outlet.

Nyampesi acknowledged the tremendous technical support his company has received from the then Fisheries Integration of Society and Habitats (FISH) project, as well as the financial support from the ongoing REFRESH project being implemented in the district.

"To process our fish, we utilize Chitofu 3 in 1, which is suitable for frying, parboiling, and smoking. Additionally, our solar tent dryer adds value to the fish by preventing contamination from flies, which can result in post-harvest losses and further infestations," Nyampesi explained to the visiting processors at his facility.

Martha Chikwezga, Treasurer of Fishland Ladies, emphasized that as a cooperative led by young women, they have received significant support from Nyampesi Fish Products Company and the Department of Fisheries. This support has enabled them to adopt new fish processing technologies that promote environmental conservation and sustainable fisheries practices.

"With our privately-owned boat, we employ men for fishing on behalf of our cooperative. After processing the fish, we sell them to Nyampesi Fish Products Company at favorable prices, and we are truly grateful for that," Chikwezga expressed to the visiting fish processors.

Bahat Stambuli, a representative of the visiting fish processors, expressed gratitude to Pact Malawi for arranging the valuable exchange visit. Stambuli highlighted the importance of learning about modern fish processing technologies, specifically mentioning the Chitofu 3 in 1. She emphasized that their cooperative would make every effort to collaborate with Nyampesi to maximize income from their fish processing business.

Neverson Msusa, the District Fisheries Officer (DFO) of Mangochi, conveyed his thanks to the REFRESH project for funding the event. He emphasized the significance of such initiatives in enabling fish processors to engage and exchange ideas on the optimal use of modern fish processing technologies, which are vital for environmental conservation and sustainable management of fisheries resources.

Funded by USAID through Pact Malawi, the REFRESH project is being implemented in Mangochi, one of the beneficiary districts, in collaboration with partner organization Community Initiative for Self-Reliance (CISER).

The aim was to provide them with an opportunity to learn and appreciate the operations of Nyampesi Fish Products Company and Fish Landladies, both located in Monkey Bay, Mangochi.

Hamis Nyampesi, the Managing Director of Nyampesi Fish Products, shared insights about his company during the exchange visit. Established in 2016, their primary objective is to purchase and process fish using environmentally-friendly processing technologies. They believe in working with youths and women, which is why they have a business partnership with Fishland Ladies.

According to Nyampesi, his company has received exceptional technical support from the previous Fisheries Integration of Society and Habitats (FISH) project, as well as financial assistance from the current REFRESH project being implemented in the district.

"We use Chitofu 3 in 1 for frying, parboiling, and smoking our fish. The solar tent dryer we have also adds value to the fish, as it prevents contamination by flies and reduces post-harvest losses," Nyampesi informed the visiting processors at his facility.

Martha Chikwezga, Treasurer of Fishland Ladies, highlighted the support they have received from Nyampesi Fish Products Company and the Department of Fisheries. This support has enabled them to adopt new fish processing technologies that promote both environmental conservation and sustainable fisheries practices.

"As a young women-led cooperative, we employ men for fishing on behalf of our cooperative. After processing the fish, we sell them to Nyampesi Fish Products Company as our market at favorable prices. We are grateful for the opportunities provided to us," said Chikwezga.

Bahat Stambuli, a representative of the visiting fish processors, expressed gratitude to Pact Malawi for organizing the exchange visit. Stambuli emphasized the importance of learning about modern fish processing technologies, specifically highlighting the value of Chitofu 3 in 1. She pledged that their cooperative would strive to strengthen their partnership with Nyampesi to maximize income from their fish processing business.

Neverson Msusa, the District Fisheries Officer (DFO) of Mangochi, thanked the REFRESH project for funding the event. He stressed the significance of such initiatives in promoting knowledge sharing and the optimal use of modern fish processing technologies for environmental conservation and sustainable fisheries management.

Funded by USAID through Pact Malawi, the REFRESH project is being implemented in Mangochi in collaboration with partner organization Community Initiative for Self-Reliance (CISER).

A two day Local Climate Adaptative Living Facility (LoCAL) mechanism training for three councils namely; Mwanza, Mulanje and Nkhota-Kota is underway in Salima district. LoCAL channels finance to decentralised local government authorities and their communities to cover the additional costs of mitigating and adapting impacts of climate change and thereby minimising and addressing potential loss and damage.

Designed in 2011 and managed by the United Nations Capital Development Fund (UNCDF) the facility supports LoCAL country-owned mechanisms for climate finance delivery that have revised adaptation solutions for more than 16 million people around the world.

More than 30 countries across Africa, Asia, the Caribbean and the Pacific have engaged with LoCAL to deliver resilience-building solutions for climate vulnerable populations.

Tawonga Mbale Luka, Director of Environmental Affairs began by saying that climate change is causing devastating impacts on all productive sectors of Malawi. The impacts we continue to observe in our communities further emphasize the high levels of vulnerability. From our policies and other strategic frameworks, it is clear that we have several challenges that are hindering our ability to build the resilience and improve the adaptive capacity of our communities, and ecosystems, and no challenge is more pronounced than the shortage of financing to implement the strategic actions that we have devised to solve some of our issues. 
 
In recognition of this challenge, as the focal point on matters of climate change management, Environmental Affairs Department has partnered with the United Nations Capital Development Fund (UNCDF) to deploy the Local Climate Adaptive Living Mechanism (LoCAL) in Malawi. The aim is to establish a Performance Based Climate Resilience Granting system to local councils to implement climate change interventions.

“The LoCAL is a mechanism that will enable us to channel climate finance to local governments. The mechanism will utilize existing Government financial management systems to empower local authorities to implement resilience building initiatives in line with district development priorities whilst also being aligned to national priorities on adaptation. LoCAL not only responds to national needs in climate change management, but is also aligned to respond to several the globally accepted Sustainable Development Goals e.g. SDG 13 on Climate Action,” said Mbale Luka in her inaugural address.
 
According to Luka, Malawi joins the group of Least Developed Countries that are utilizing the LoCAL to address significant financial challenges within the climate change space. This training session has therefore been organized with the intention to create awareness of the LoCAL Climate Adaptive Living Facility, its application in Malawi as well as discuss proposals for utilisation of Performance Based Climate Resilient Grant (PBCRG).
 
 
Yusuf Mkungula, Principal Secretary of Natural Resources and Climate Change made emphasis that the intensity and occurrence of extreme weather events continues to devastate people’s lives and livelihoods, ecosystems and on the country’s economy in general. 

Mkungula cited for instance, earlier this year, when Malawi was ravaged by flooding, mudslides, and heavy rains compounded by strong winds as cyclone Freddy tore through many districts in the southern region of Malawi. The Cyclone affected over 2 million Malawians with the estimated monetary loss amounting to $506.7 million. As a country, such 
 
     “As a Least Developed Country, Malawi’s financial and technical ability to respond to the impacts of extreme weather events is limited. Through various policies and strategic          
    frameworks i.e. the Malawi 2063, National Resilience Strategy, National Climate Change Management Policy, and Nationally Determined Contributions (NDCs), we have, together,  designed various measures to respond accordingly to build the resilience and adaptive capacity of the vulnerable communities and ecosystems,” stressed Mkungula in his address.
 ‘
He suggested a need to engage innovative sources of climate finance if the country is to deploy the necessary actions. 

‘For instance, the Malawi NDC Report has estimated Malawi requires an estimated external and domestic financial support to the tune of forty-six billion United States Dollars ($46, 000,000,000), to fully implement the priority measures listed in the NDC. I am sure that you will agree with me that indeed this is a significant amount and will therefore require external support to ensure that the set goals are met. We need financing that is accessible and adequate to meet the needs,” observed Mkungula adding the this led to 


Government has partnered with the United Nations Capital Development Fund (UNCDF) to implement the Local Climate Adaptive Living (LoCAL) Facility Project which will 			ensure that selected communities in the target project areas in the country, namely, Nkhota-kota, Mwanza and Mulanje districts are equipped to respond to the adverse impacts 	cand also to be resilient against future occurrences.

A two-day training on the Local Climate Adaptive Living Facility (LoCAL) mechanism is currently taking place in Salima district, involving three councils - Mwanza, Mulanje, and Nkhota-Kota. The LoCAL mechanism provides financial support to decentralized local government authorities and their communities to address the additional costs of mitigating and adapting to the impacts of climate change, thereby reducing potential loss and damage.

Managed by the United Nations Capital Development Fund (UNCDF) since 2011, LoCAL supports country-owned mechanisms for climate finance delivery that have provided adaptation solutions to over 16 million people worldwide. In Africa, Asia, the Caribbean, and the Pacific, more than 30 countries have engaged with LoCAL to implement resilience-building measures for vulnerable populations affected by climate change.

During the training, Tawonga Mbale Luka, Director of Environmental Affairs, emphasized the devastating impacts of climate change on Malawi's productive sectors. The country faces numerous challenges hindering the resilience-building and adaptive capacity improvement of communities and ecosystems, with insufficient financing being one of the most significant hurdles. To address this challenge, the Environmental Affairs Department has partnered with UNCDF to implement LoCAL in Malawi. The aim is to establish a Performance-Based Climate Resilience Granting system for local councils to implement climate change interventions.

Mbale Luka explained that LoCAL enables the channeling of climate finance to local governments, utilizing existing government financial management systems. This empowers local authorities to implement resilience-building initiatives aligned with district development priorities and national adaptation goals, such as SDG 13 on Climate Action.

Malawi joins the group of Least Developed Countries utilizing LoCAL to address financial challenges in the climate change space. The training session is designed to raise awareness about the LoCAL Climate Adaptive Living Facility, its application in Malawi, as well as discuss proposals for utilizing the Performance-Based Climate Resilient Grant (PBCRG).

Yusuf Mkungula, Principal Secretary of Natural Resources and Climate Change, highlighted the ongoing devastating impacts of extreme weather events on people's lives, livelihoods, ecosystems, and the country's economy. He cited the recent cyclone Freddy and its aftermath, which caused significant damage and affected over two million Malawians, with estimated monetary losses reaching $506.7 million. As a Least Developed Country, Malawi's financial and technical capacity to respond to these impacts is limited.

Mkungula stressed the need to engage innovative sources of climate finance to effectively implement necessary actions. For instance, the Malawi NDC Report estimates that the country requires approximately $46 billion in external and domestic financial support to fully implement priority measures outlined in the Nationally Determined Contributions (NDC). He emphasized the necessity for accessible and adequate financing to meet these needs.

In response to this, the government has partnered with UNCDF to implement the LoCAL Facility Project, ensuring that communities in targeted areas - Nkhota-Kota, Mwanza, and Mulanje districts - are equipped to respond to adverse climate impacts and build resilience for the future.
